title: Shower Chair vs. Transfer Bench: Which Is Safer for Seniors?
description: A plain-language comparison of shower chairs and transfer benches, with clear guidance on which is right for your bathroom and mobility level.
summary: Choose a shower chair if your senior can safely step over the tub wall or uses a walk-in shower. Choose a transfer bench if stepping over the tub edge is risky — the bench lets them sit and slide across instead.
verdictA: Best when stepping into the shower is already safe and space is tight.
verdictB: Best when stepping over a tub wall is the main fall risk.

points.label: Tub entry safety
points.a: Requires stepping over the tub wall
points.b: Eliminates stepping over the wall — sit and slide

points.label: Footprint
points.a: Compact, fits inside the tub or stall

title: Medical Alert Pendant vs. Smartwatch: Which Should a Senior Wear?
description: Compare classic medical alert pendants with smartwatch-style alert devices on battery life, fall detection, comfort, and stigma.
summary: Pick a pendant-style mobile unit for the longest battery life and optional automatic fall detection. Pick a smartwatch-style device if your senior resists wearing an obvious medical pendant and will reliably charge it daily.
verdictA: Best for battery life and fall detection.

title: Bed Rails vs. Bedside Handles: Which Helps Seniors More?
description: Understand the difference between full-length bed rails and compact bedside handles, and which is safer for getting in and out of bed.
summary: A full-length bed rail offers more surface to grip and can help prevent rolling out of bed. A compact bedside handle (assist rail) is less obtrusive and best for seniors who mainly need a sturdy handhold when sitting up and standing.
verdictA: Best for roll-out prevention and a long gripping surface.
verdictB: Best for a discreet handhold when standing up.

title: Raised Toilet Seat vs. Toilet Safety Frame: Which Does a Senior Need?
description: Compare bolt-on raised toilet seats with freestanding toilet safety frames on height, support, and whether you can modify the toilet.
summary: Choose a raised toilet seat when you want to add height and can attach to the toilet — it reduces how far the senior lowers and lifts. Choose a freestanding safety frame when you can't modify the toilet (renters) or you mainly need sturdy support arms around the existing toilet.
verdictA: Best for adding height with secure, attached arms.
verdictB: Best when you can't modify the toilet and want support arms.

points.label: Adds seat height
points.b: Not by itself — adds support, not height

title: Corded vs. Cordless Phone for Seniors: Which Is Safer?
description: Compare corded and amplified cordless phones for seniors on reliability, hearing support, and whole-home convenience.
summary: A corded phone never needs charging and keeps working in a power outage, so it's the most reliable choice for a fixed spot near where the senior sits. An amplified cordless phone offers louder sound and whole-home mobility. Many families keep one of each.
verdictA: Best for outage-proof reliability in a fixed location.
verdictB: Best for whole-home use and the loudest amplification.

points.label: Amplification
points.b: Up to 50 dB boost + loud ringer

title: Mobile Medical Alert vs. Caregiver Pager: Which Should You Buy?
description: Compare a monitored mobile medical alert with a no-monthly-fee caregiver pager, and learn which fits a senior's living situation.
summary: Choose a monitored mobile medical alert when the senior lives alone or goes out — it connects to a 24/7 response center that can dispatch help anywhere. Choose a no-fee caregiver pager only when someone is usually home: it alerts that caregiver, but does not call 911.
verdictA: Best for living alone — summons professional help anywhere.
verdictB: Best when a caregiver is home — no monthly fee.
